title: Optimizing React Memoized Component Re-renders Due to Changing Object Properties

statement.markdown: You have a React functional component that is memoized using the `useMemo` hook. The memoized value is an object that is created based on the component's props. However, you notice that even though the props haven't changed, the component is re-rendering due to the changing object properties. Your task is to fix the bug by optimizing the memoization to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an object with two properties: `props` and `state`. The `props` object will contain several keys, and the `state` object will also contain several keys.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the memoized value, which is an object that is created based on the `props` and `state` objects.
statement.notes: The function should handle cases where the `props` or `state` objects are deeply nested and contain complex data structures.

constraints.description: The number of properties in the `props` object
The number of properties in the `state` object

examples.explanation: The memoized value is created based on the `props` object. Since the `props` object hasn't changed, the memoized value remains the same.
The `state` object has changed, but the `props` object remains the same. The memoized value remains the same.
The `state` object is empty, so the memoized value is based solely on the `props` object.

hints.text: When using `useMemo`, make sure to provide a correct dependency array to prevent unnecessary re-renders.

editorial.title: Optimizing React Memoization
editorial.intuition: To optimize the memoization, we need to make sure that the memoized value is recreated only when the `props` object changes. We can use the `JSON.stringify` function to compare the `props` objects and determine whether they have changed.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute-force solution is to recursively traverse the `props` object and compare each property. However, this approach can be inefficient for deeply nested objects.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the `JSON.stringify` function to compare the `props` objects. This approach is more efficient and accurate.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `JSON.stringify` function to compare the `props` objects and create the memoized value only when the `props` object changes.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the fact that `JSON.stringify` returns a string representation of the object. If the `props` object changes, the string representation will also change, and the memoized value will be recreated.
commonMistakes: Not providing a correct dependency array to `useMemo`
Not using `JSON.stringify` to compare the `props` objects
